items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, why are special use frequencies assigned such that adjacent ARTCCs do not have the same frequency?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'Special use frequencies are assigned to ARTCCs in such a manner that adjacent ARTCCs will not have the same frequency' to prevent interference and confusion across ARTCC boundaries.

choices.text: To allow aircraft to use the same frequency across multiple ARTCCs without frequency changes.
To prevent adjacent ARTCCs from having the same frequency, avoiding interference and confusion across boundaries.
To ensure each ARTCC can assign the frequency to two sectors simultaneously.
Because FAA regulations prohibit frequency reuse within 500 NM of another ARTCC.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, what special provision applies to formations of five or more USAF aircraft deploying to a staging base or overseas?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-15a1, 'Formations of five or more USAF aircraft deploying either to a continental U.S. staging base or nonstop to an overseas location are authorized to use special use frequencies at any time.'

choices.text: They are authorized to use special use frequencies only during night operations.
They are authorized to use special use frequencies at any time.
They must use the special use frequency as backup only.
They are required to use standard ARTCC frequencies for all deployments.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, about how far from the ARCP does a receiver aircraft need to leave the special use frequency for tanker communications during aerial refueling?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'This will occur when the receiver is approximately 200 miles from the ARCP. The tanker aircraft will remain on the ARTCC assigned frequency and will relay clearances to the receiver as required.'

choices.text: Approximately 50 miles from the ARCP.
Approximately 100 miles from the ARCP.
Approximately 200 miles from the ARCP.
Approximately 300 miles from the ARCP.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, what is the static call sign used by B-57 aircraft engaged in pressure suit operations?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'B-57 aircraft engaged in pressure suit operations will use the static call sign KITE.'

choices.text: PRESSURE.
KITE.
HAWK.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-16, what must a controller tell a pilot when the airfield of intended landing lies within a reported or forecast area of hazardous radiation?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16b, 'Inform pilots when an airfield of intended landing lies within a reported or forecasted area of hazardous radiation and request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.'

choices.text: Inform the pilot and request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.
Automatically reroute the aircraft to an alternate airport outside the radiation area.
Close the airfield to all operations until the radiation hazard clears.
Issue a NOTAM and wait for the pilot to request alternate landing instructions.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-16, what should a controller do when a pilot's proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16a, 'Advise pilots whenever their proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecasted area of hazardous radiation and reroute the aircraft when requested by the pilot.' The advice is mandatory; the reroute is upon pilot request.

choices.text: Advise the pilot and reroute the aircraft when requested by the pilot.
Automatically reroute all aircraft without consulting the pilot.
Advise the pilot but never reroute because radiation areas are not ATC concerns.
Only advise the pilot if the radiation hazard is expected to last more than 2 hours.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-17, what missions are SAMP call sign aircraft engaged in?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, SAMP aircraft are 'engaged in aerial sampling/surveying missions for nuclear, chemical, or hazardous material contamination.'

choices.text: Weather reconnaissance and hurricane tracking.
Aerial sampling/surveying for nuclear, chemical, or hazardous material contamination.
Aerial refueling and formation flight operations.
Flight inspection of NAVAIDs and radar calibration.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-17, what may be done with other IFR aircraft to accommodate SAMP flight requests?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, 'Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that requests by SAMP aircraft are approved.' This gives controllers authority to adjust other traffic to prioritize SAMP missions.

choices.text: Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that requests by SAMP aircraft are approved.
Other IFR aircraft must be held until the SAMP mission is complete.
Other IFR aircraft cannot be affected; SAMP aircraft must fit into existing traffic flow.
Only VFR aircraft may be recleared; IFR aircraft maintain priority over SAMP flights.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-18, how are AWACS/NORAD Special flights identified in the flight plan?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'To identify these flights, the words "AWACS/NORAD SPECIAL" will be included as the first item in the remarks section of the flight plan.'

choices.text: By a special transponder code assigned in the flight plan.
By the words 'AWACS/NORAD SPECIAL' as the first item in the flight plan remarks section.
By the call sign prefix 'NORA' in the aircraft identification.
By a separate NOTAM filed 24 hours before the flight.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-18, what en route altitude change is acceptable while expediting an AWACS/NORAD Special flight?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-18a, 'En route altitude changes +/- 2,000 feet from the requested flight level' are acceptable.

choices.text: +/- 5,000 feet from the requested flight level.
+/- 1,000 feet from the requested flight level.
+/- 2,000 feet from the requested flight level.
+/- 4,000 feet from the requested flight level.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-19, who bears the responsibility to delay release of dropsondes if traffic is a factor?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'It is the aircraft commanders responsibility to delay release of dropsondes if traffic is a factor. Aircraft commanders will delay release of dropsondes based solely upon traffic as issued by ATC.'

choices.text: The aircraft commander.
The air traffic controller overseeing the sector.
The supervisory traffic management coordinator-in-charge.
The NOAA or Air Force mission coordinator on the ground.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-19, what is the difference between NWSOP missions and NHOP missions?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-19, TEAL and NOAA aircraft gather meteorological data on 'winter storms (NWSOP missions), hurricanes and tropical cyclones (NHOP missions).' NWSOP = winter storms, NHOP = hurricanes/tropical cyclones.

choices.text: NWSOP missions gather data on winter storms; NHOP missions gather data on hurricanes and tropical cyclones.
NWSOP missions are NOAA-only; NHOP missions are Air Force-only.
NWSOP missions operate below FL 300; NHOP missions operate above FL 300.
There is no operational difference; the terms refer to the same type of mission.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-19, what three items must a traffic advisory include when a dropsonde release time is received from a TEAL or NOAA mission aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-19a, the traffic advisory must include: '1. Altitude. 2. Direction of flight. 3. ETA at the point closest to drop area (or at the fix/intersection where drop will occur).'

choices.text: Altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the point closest to the drop area or fix/intersection where drop will occur.
Aircraft type, altitude, and estimated time of departure from the area.
Call sign, transponder code, and requested altitude after the drop.
Altitude, speed, and heading of all traffic within 10 NM of the drop area.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-20, what does the evasive action maneuver consist of?
items.explanation: Per the note, it is performed at or above FL 250 along a 60 NM segment, including: flying a zigzag pattern on both left and right sides of the flight plan route centerline, with altitude deviations made in conjunction with lateral maneuvering.

choices.text: A rapid climb to a higher altitude followed by an immediate descent back to the assigned flight level.
Flying a zigzag pattern on both sides of the route centerline with altitude deviations, at or above FL 250 along a 60 NM segment.
A series of 360-degree turns at the assigned altitude to simulate evading a threat.
A straight-line acceleration to supersonic speed for a 60 NM segment.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-20, what is the maximum lateral deviation from the route centerline during an evasive action maneuver?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'Lateral deviations from the route centerline will not normally exceed 12 miles.'

choices.text: 5 miles.
25 miles.
12 miles.
20 miles.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-20, what is the total altitude block within which the evasive action maneuver must be confined?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'Altitude variations must not exceed plus or minus 1,000 feet of the assigned flight level; i.e., confined within a 2,000 foot block.'

choices.text: A 1,000 foot block (+/- 500 feet).
A 2,000 foot block (+/- 1,000 feet).
A 4,000 foot block (+/- 2,000 feet).
A 5,000 foot block (+/- 2,500 feet).

items.question_text: Under 9-2-21, what is the clearance limit for aircraft operating in a nonstandard formation/cell?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-21e, 'Clear aircraft operating in a nonstandard formation/cell to the breakup fix as the clearance limit.' Data beyond the breakup point is forwarded as routine flight plan information.

choices.text: The destination airport.
The breakup fix.
The ARTCC boundary.
The first fix after the breakup point.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-21, what must a controller do if a breakup cannot be approved when the formation reaches the breakup fix?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-21f, 'If a breakup cannot be approved, issue an appropriate clearance for the flight to continue as a formation.'

choices.text: Hold the formation at the breakup fix until breakup can be approved.
Issue a clearance for the flight to continue as a formation.
Cancel the formation clearance and require each aircraft to file individually.
Vector each aircraft individually away from the formation route.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-21, what intra-cell vertical spacing must be assigned between each aircraft in an approved nonstandard formation/cell?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-21b, 'controllers must assign sufficient altitudes to allow intra-cell vertical spacing of 500 feet between each aircraft in the formation.'

choices.text: 1,000 feet.
500 feet.
2,000 feet.
250 feet.

items.situation: A pilot's filed route passes through a forecast hazardous radiation area. The pilot has not yet requested a reroute. Another controller says the pilot doesn't need to be told because the radiation hazard may dissipate before the aircraft arrives.
items.question_text: What action must you take regarding the radiation area?

choices.text: Advise the pilot that the proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area and reroute if the pilot requests it.
Automatically reroute the aircraft without advising the pilot to save time.
Wait to see if the hazard dissipates and advise only if it persists within 50 NM of the aircraft.
Take no action because radiation hazards are the pilot's responsibility under 14 CFR part 91.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-16a, advise pilots whenever their proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area and reroute when requested. The advice is mandatory regardless of whether the pilot might already know.
items.para_context: AVOIDANCE OF AREAS OF NUCLEAR RADIATION

items.situation: A SAMP call sign aircraft requests an altitude change and a route deviation to conduct aerial sampling. Your sector has several IFR aircraft on conflicting routes. Another controller suggests denying the SAMP request to avoid reclearing other traffic.
items.question_text: How should the SAMP aircraft request be handled?

choices.text: Approve the SAMP requests to the maximum extent possible; other IFR aircraft may be recleared to accommodate the SAMP mission.
Deny the request because SAMP flights have no priority over other IFR traffic.
Approve only the altitude change and deny the route deviation because dual requests cannot both be approved.
Ask the SAMP aircraft to hold at its present position until all other IFR aircraft have cleared the area.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, SAMP aircraft receive special handling, and inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes are approved to the maximum extent possible. Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that SAMP requests are approved.

items.question_text: Under what condition may a controller approve a pilot's request for an evasive action maneuver?

choices.text: Only on the basis of a permissible traffic situation.
Any time the pilot requests it, because evasive action maneuvers are pilot-discretion operations.
Only when the aircraft is operating in restricted airspace or a MOA.
Only after coordinating with the DEN and receiving NORAD concurrence.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-20, 'Approve a pilot request to conduct an evasive action maneuver only on the basis of a permissible traffic situation.' The controller evaluates whether traffic permits the maneuver before approving.

items.situation: A nonstandard formation/cell has been approved and is approaching the breakup fix in your sector. After breakup, the aircraft need individual clearances to different destinations. Another controller suggests just clearing them to their destinations now instead of to the breakup fix.
items.question_text: What is the correct clearance limit for this formation?

choices.text: Clear the formation to the breakup fix as the clearance limit; forward route/altitude data beyond the breakup point as routine flight plan information.
Clear each aircraft individually to its destination before the breakup occurs.
Clear to the ARTCC boundary and let the next center handle the breakup.
Clear to the first fix beyond the breakup point to minimize frequency changes.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-21e, clear to the breakup fix as the clearance limit. Data beyond the breakup point is forwarded as routine flight plan information to the center concerned. At breakup, issue appropriate clearances to authorize transition from formation to individual routes (9-2-21f).
